The ***Kojiki*** (古事記, "Records of Ancient Matters" or "An Account of Ancient Matters"), also sometimes read as ***Furukotofumi***[[1]](#cite_note-1) or ***Furukotobumi***,[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[a]](#cite_note-3) is an early [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") chronicle of [myths](/wiki/Myth "Myth"), legends, [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn"), genealogies, [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"), and semi-historical accounts dating as far back as 641[[3]](#cite_note-4) concerning the origin of the Japanese [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago"), the *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")*, and the [Japanese imperial line](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan"). It is claimed in its preface to have been composed by [Ō no Yasumaro](/wiki/%C5%8C_no_Yasumaro "Ō no Yasumaro") at the request of [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei") in the early 8th century (711–712), and thus is usually considered to be the oldest extant literary work in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[4]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-6)

The myths contained in the *Kojiki* as well as the *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")* are part of the inspiration behind many practices and unified "Shinto orthodoxy".[[6]](#cite_note-7) Later, they were incorporated into [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") practices such as the *[misogi](/wiki/Misogi "Misogi")* purification ritual.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-e-9)[[9]](#cite_note-d-10)

## Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Composition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Ono-Yasumaro.jpg/250px-Ono-Yasumaro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ono-Yasumaro.jpg)

Portrait of [Ō no Yasumaro](/wiki/%C5%8C_no_Yasumaro "Ō no Yasumaro") by [Kikuchi Yōsai](/wiki/Kikuchi_Y%C5%8Dsai "Kikuchi Yōsai") (19th century)

It is believed that the compilation of various genealogical and anecdotal histories of the imperial (Yamato) court and prominent clans began during the reigns of [Emperors Keitai](/wiki/Emperor_Keitai "Emperor Keitai") and [Kinmei](/wiki/Emperor_Kinmei "Emperor Kinmei") in the 6th century, with the first concerted effort at historical compilation of which we have record being the one made in 620 under the auspices of [Prince Shotoku](/wiki/Prince_Shotoku "Prince Shotoku") and [Soga no Umako](/wiki/Soga_no_Umako "Soga no Umako"). According to the *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")*, the documents compiled under their initiative were the *[Tennōki](/wiki/Tenn%C5%8Dki "Tennōki")* or the "Record of the Emperors", the *Kokki* (国記, also *Kunitsufumi*) or the "National Record", and other "fundamental records" (本記, *hongi* or *mototsufumi*) pertaining to influential clans and free subjects. Out of these texts, only the *Kokki* survived the burning of [Soga no Emishi](/wiki/Soga_no_Emishi "Soga no Emishi")'s estate (where these documents were kept) during the [Isshi incident](/wiki/Isshi_incident "Isshi incident") of 645, and was itself apparently lost soon after.[[10]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki-11)

The *Kojiki*'s preface indicates that leading families also kept their own historical and genealogical records; indeed, one of the reasons it gives for the compilation of the *Kojiki* is the correction of errors that had supposedly crept into these documents. According to the preface, [Emperor Tenmu](/wiki/Emperor_Tenmu "Emperor Tenmu") (reigned 673–686) ordered the review and emendation of clan documents and commissioned a certain court attendant (*toneri*) of exceptional memory named [Hieda no Are](/wiki/Hieda_no_Are "Hieda no Are") to memorize records and [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") concerning the imperial lineage. Beyond this memorization, nothing occurred until the reign of [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei") (reigned 707–715), who on the 18th of the 9th month of 711 ([Wadō](/wiki/Wad%C5%8D_(era) "Wadō (era)") 4) ordered the courtier [Ō no Yasumaro](/wiki/%C5%8C_no_Yasumaro "Ō no Yasumaro") to record what had been learned by Hieda no Are. Yasumaro wrote down the information in [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") and presented his work to Empress Genmei on the 28th of the 1st month of 712 (Wadō 5).[[4]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[10]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki-11)

### Purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Purpose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_%28%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98%29.png/250px-Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_%28%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98%29.png)](/wiki/File:Shinpukuji-bon_Kojiki_(%E7%9C%9F%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA%E6%9C%AC%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98).png)

A page from the Shinpukuji manuscript of the *Kojiki*, dating from 1371–72

The *Kojiki* is a collation of different traditions woven into a single "official" mythology, made in an attempt to justify the rule of the imperial Yamato [polity](/wiki/Polity "Polity") and at the same time to subsume different interest groups under its wing by giving them a place and an interest in the national genealogy-mythology. Apart from furthering the imperial agenda, an increased interest in the nation's origins in reaction to the influx of foreign culture and the need for an authoritative genealogical account by which to consider the claims of noble families and to reorganize them into a new system of ranks and titles are also possible factors for its compilation.[[11]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki2-12)

The *Kojiki*'s narrative establishes the [Yamato line](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan")'s right to rule via myth and legend, portraying it as the progeny of heavenly deities and the rightful heir to the land of Japan. A good part of the latter portion of the text is spent recounting various genealogies which served not only to give the imperial family an air of antiquity (which may not necessarily reflect historical reality), but also served to tie, whether true or not, many existing clans' genealogies to their own. Regardless of the work's original intent, it finalized and possibly even formulated the framework by which Japanese history was examined in terms of the reign of emperors.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-6)[[11]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki2-12)

In contrast to the *Nihon Shoki* (compiled 720), the first of [six histories](/wiki/Rikkokushi "Rikkokushi") commissioned by the imperial court, which was modeled on [Chinese dynastic histories](/wiki/Twenty-Four_Histories "Twenty-Four Histories") and was intended to be a national chronicle that could be shown with pride to foreign envoys, the *Kojiki* is inward looking, concerned mainly with the ruling family and prominent clans, and is apparently intended for internal consumption. Whereas the *Nihon Shoki* uses a variety of source documents (including Chinese texts), the *Kojiki* is apparently based on sources handed down within the court.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## Transmission and study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Transmission and study")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Kojiki_kan-ei-ban.jpg/250px-Kojiki_kan-ei-ban.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kojiki_kan-ei-ban.jpg)

Kan'ei Kojiki, 1644 ([Kokugakuin University](/wiki/Kokugakuin_University "Kokugakuin University"))

Whereas the *Nihon Shoki*, owing to its status as one of the six imperial histories, was widely read and studied during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185), the *Kojiki* was mostly treated as an ancillary text. Indeed, a work known as the *[Sendai Kuji Hongi](/wiki/Sendai_Kuji_Hongi "Sendai Kuji Hongi")* (also known as the *Kujiki*), claimed to have been authored by Prince Shōtoku and Soga no Umako, was considered to be earlier and more reliable than the *Kojiki*. (Modern scholarly consensus holds the *Kuji Hongi* to be a Heian period forgery based on both the *Kojiki* and the *Shoki*, although certain portions may indeed preserve genuine early traditions and sources.) By the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") (1185–1333), the work languished in obscurity such that very few people had access to the text, particularly that of the middle (second) volume.[[15]](#cite_note-16) It is due to this neglect that the *Kojiki* is available only in comparatively late manuscripts, the earliest of which dates to the late 14th century.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)

It was with the advent of printing in the early modern period that the *Kojiki* first reached a wide audience. The earliest printed edition of the text was the *Kan'ei Kojiki* (寛永古事記), published in Kyoto in 1644 ([Kan'ei](/wiki/Kan%27ei "Kan'ei") 21). A second edition, the *Gōtō Kojiki* (鼇頭古事記, "*Kojiki* with Marginal Notes") was printed by [Deguchi (Watarai) Nobuyoshi](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%87%BA%E5%8F%A3%E5%BB%B6%E4%BD%B3 "ja:出口延佳"), a priest at [Ise Shrine](/wiki/Ise_Shrine "Ise Shrine"), in 1687 ([Jōkyō](/wiki/J%C5%8Dky%C5%8D "Jōkyō") 4).[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Kojiki_den.jpg/330px-Kojiki_den.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kojiki_den.jpg)

*Kojiki-den* by Motoori Norinaga

The birth of nativist studies (*[kokugaku](/wiki/Kokugaku "Kokugaku")*) and nationalist sentiment during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") saw a reappraisal of the *Kojiki*. *Kokugaku* scholars saw Japan's earliest writings as the repository of a uniquely superior Japanese identity that could be revived by recovering the ancient language they were written in; the *Kojiki*, by virtue of its antiquity, gained the status of a sacred text.[[18]](#cite_note-heldt-19) The *Kojiki* came to be highly regarded that scholars such as [Kada no Azumamaro](/wiki/Kada_no_Azumamaro "Kada no Azumamaro") and [Kamo no Mabuchi](/wiki/Kamo_no_Mabuchi "Kamo no Mabuchi") – himself a student of Azumamaro – produced annotated versions of it.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Motoori_Norinaga_self_portrait.jpg/250px-Motoori_Norinaga_self_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motoori_Norinaga_self_portrait.jpg)

[Motoori Norinaga](/wiki/Motoori_Norinaga "Motoori Norinaga")

The *Kojiki* received its most serious study and exposition in the hands of [Motoori Norinaga](/wiki/Motoori_Norinaga "Motoori Norinaga"), who obtained a copy of the Kan'ei printed edition in 1754. After meeting Mabuchi in 1763, Norinaga began to devote his efforts to an in-depth scholarly study of the text. A monumental 44-volume study of the *Kojiki* called *[Kojiki-den](/wiki/Kojiki-den "Kojiki-den")*, composed over a 34-year period (1764–1798), was the fruit of his labor.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17) With Norinaga, the *Kojiki* assumed an importance equal to the *Nihon Shoki*; in fact, in his view the *Kojiki* was a more trustworthy source for ancient Japanese traditions than was the *Shoki* precisely because it was free of "Chinese mentality". He viewed the *Kojiki* as a true account of actual events that when read correctly, could reveal Japan in its pristine, ideal state as a community where the *kami*, the emperor and the people lived in harmony.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Norinaga's work was carried on in different directions by his disciple [Hirata Atsutane](/wiki/Hirata_Atsutane "Hirata Atsutane") and his rivals Fujitani Mitsue (1768–1823)[[20]](#cite_note-21) and Tachibana Moribe (1781–1849),[[21]](#cite_note-22) who each produced commentaries and treatises on the text.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)

The *Kojiki* became once more the object of scholarly focus and discussion in the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") with the introduction of Western academic disciplines such as [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology") and [comparative mythology](/wiki/Comparative_mythology "Comparative mythology"). The importance of the text as a work of literary value was recognized, and scholars realized that its accounts were comparable in many ways to [ancient Greek](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") and [Roman myths](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology"). At the same time, however, the *Kojiki* and *Nihon Shoki* achieved a sort of scriptural status under [State Shintō](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto"), which viewed the stories contained therein as orthodox national history. Official ideology upheld as unquestionable fact the belief in the emperor's divinity and the idea of Japan as a racially superior "national body" (*[kokutai](/wiki/Kokutai "Kokutai")*), with scholars who questioned their veracity facing the threat of censorship, forced resignation, or even trial in court.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)[[18]](#cite_note-heldt-19)

Until the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era"), the text's sacred nature was not known to have been questioned.[[22]](#cite_note-Myths_and_Legends-23) However, the *Kojiki* was still widely seen as inferior to the *Nihon Shoki* until [Motoori Norinaga](/wiki/Motoori_Norinaga "Motoori Norinaga") wrote the *[Kojiki-den](/wiki/Kojiki-den "Kojiki-den")*.[[22]](#cite_note-Myths_and_Legends-23) In 1913, [Tsuda Sōkichi](/wiki/S%C5%8Dkichi_Tsuda "Sōkichi Tsuda") argued in a study that the *Kojiki*, particularly in its earlier sections, was neither history nor myth but a document created to legitimize the rule of the imperial line. While his conclusions led to considerable controversy, his influence remains in subsequent studies of the text (particularly in post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") scholarship), which amounts largely to development and correction of the line of thought originally proposed by him. In reaction to Tsuda, [Watsuji Tetsurō](/wiki/Tetsuro_Watsuji "Tetsuro Watsuji") (1920) argued for a literary appreciation of the Kojiki, claiming that this gave it inner coherence. [Kurano Kenji](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%80%89%E9%87%8E%E6%86%B2%E5%8F%B8 "ja:倉野憲司") (1927) took it a step further, proposing that the *Kojiki* may best be compared with Western [epic literature](/wiki/Epic_literature "Epic literature") and regarded as a national epic like *[Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf")* is in the English-speaking world. During the 1920s and 30s, linguist [Hashimoto Shinkichi](/wiki/Shinkichi_Hashimoto "Shinkichi Hashimoto") studied the phonology of the [Old Japanese language](/wiki/Old_Japanese "Old Japanese"), and his conclusions were applied by scholars to the study of the text.[[16]](#cite_note-Philippi-Kojiki3-17)

The *Kojiki* continued to attract the attention of academics and other specialists in the post-war period, which saw the appearance of numerous editions, translations and commentaries on the text by authors such as Kurano Kenji, [Takeda Yūkichi](/wiki/Y%C5%ABkichi_Takeda "Yūkichi Takeda"), [Saigō Nobutsuna](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E8%A5%BF%E9%83%B7%E4%BF%A1%E7%B6%B1 "ja:西郷信綱"), and [Kōnoshi Takamitsu](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%A5%9E%E9%87%8E%E5%BF%97%E9%9A%86%E5%85%89 "ja:神野志隆光").

### Manuscripts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Manuscripts")]

There are two major branches of *Kojiki* manuscripts: Ise and Urabe. The extant Urabe branch consists of 36 existing manuscripts all based on the 1522 copies by Urabe Kanenaga. The Ise branch may be subdivided into the Shinpukuji-bon (真福寺本) manuscript of 1371–1372 and the Dōka-bon (道果本) manuscripts. The Dōka sub-branch consists of:

* the Dōka-bon (道果本) manuscript of 1381; only the first half of the first volume remains
* the Dōshō-bon (道祥本) manuscript of 1424; only the first volume remains, and there are many defects
* the Shun'yu-bon (春瑜本) manuscript of 1426; one volume

The Shinpukuji-bon manuscript (1371–1372) is the oldest existing manuscript. While divided into the Ise branch, it is actually a mixture of the two branches. The monk Ken'yu based his copy on Ōnakatomi Sadayo's copy. In 1266, Sadayo copied volumes one and three but did not have access to the second volume. Finally, in 1282, he obtained access to the second volume through a Urabe-branch manuscript that he used to transcribe.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Structure")]

The *Kojiki* recounts the mythological creation of Japan, the genealogy of the Japanese gods (*kami*), and the histories of the semi-legendary early emperors of Japan. Its narrative portions also contain dozens of short songs and poems (*uta*), often spoken by gods, heroes, or early rules. The book is written mostly in Classical Chinese, although the songs are written in [Old Japanese](/wiki/Old_Japanese "Old Japanese") using *[man'yōgana](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dgana "Man'yōgana")*, a system where [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") were used purely phonetically to represent the syllables of Old Japanese.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-24)

### Sections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sections")]

The *Kojiki* is divided into three parts: the *Kamitsumaki* (上巻, *"upper volume"*), the *Nakatsumaki* (中巻; "middle volume") and the *Shimotsumaki* (下巻; "lower volume").

* The *Kamitsumaki*, also known as the *Kamiyo no Maki* (神代巻; "Volume of the Age of the Gods"), includes the [preface](/wiki/Preface "Preface") of the *Kojiki*, and is focused on the deities of creation and the births of various deities of the *kamiyo* (神代) period, or [Age of the Gods](/wiki/Age_of_the_Gods "Age of the Gods"). The *Kamitsumaki* also outlines the myths concerning the foundation of Japan. It describes how [Ninigi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Ninigi-no-Mikoto "Ninigi-no-Mikoto"), grandson of [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu") and great-grandfather of [Emperor Jimmu](/wiki/Emperor_Jimmu "Emperor Jimmu"), descended from [heaven](/wiki/Takamagahara "Takamagahara") to Takachihonomine in [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB "Kyūshū") and became the [progenitor](/wiki/Ancestor "Ancestor") of the Japanese Imperial line.[[8]](#cite_note-e-9)[[9]](#cite_note-d-10)[[24]](#cite_note-25)
* The *Nakatsumaki* begins with the conquests of [Jimmu](/wiki/Emperor_Jimmu "Emperor Jimmu"), which make him the first [Emperor of Japan](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan"); and ends with the 15th Emperor, [Ōjin](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cjin "Emperor Ōjin"). The [second](/wiki/Emperor_Suizei "Emperor Suizei") through [ninth](/wiki/Emperor_Kaika "Emperor Kaika") Emperors' reigns are recorded in a minimum of detail, with only their names, the names of their various descendants, and the locations of their palaces and tombs listed, with no mention of their achievements. Many of the stories in this volume are mythological; the [allegedly historical information](/wiki/Historiography_of_Japan "Historiography of Japan") is highly suspect.
* The *Shimotsumaki* covers the 16th to 33rd Emperors and, unlike previous volumes, has very limited references to interactions with deities. (Such interactions are very prominent in the first and second volumes.) Information about the 24th to 33rd Emperors is scant.

## Synopsis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Synopsis")]

What follows is... a condensed summary of the contents of the text, including many of the names of gods, emperors, and locations as well as events which took place in association with them. The original Japanese is included in parentheses where appropriate.

### Preface (序)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Preface (序)")]

[Ō no Yasumaro](/wiki/%C5%8C_no_Yasumaro "Ō no Yasumaro")'s preface, in the form of a dedicatory address to [Empress Genmei](/wiki/Empress_Genmei "Empress Genmei"), begins with a poem summarizing the main contents of the work. He then relates how [Emperor Tenmu](/wiki/Emperor_Tenmu "Emperor Tenmu") commissioned [Hieda no Are](/wiki/Hieda_no_Are "Hieda no Are") to memorize the genealogies and records of the imperial house years earlier, and how Genmei in turn ordered Yasumaro to compile a written record of what Are had learned. He finally concludes the preface with a brief explanation of the [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") used to [transcribe](/wiki/Man%27yogana "Man'yogana") native Japanese words in the text and the division of the work into three volumes.

### The Kamitsumaki (上巻), or first volume

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kojiki&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Kamitsumaki (上巻), or first volume")]

[The Beginning of Heaven and Earth](/wiki/Japanese_creation_myth "Japanese creation myth")
:   When heaven and earth came into existence, three gods collectively known as the *[Kotoamatsukami](/wiki/Kotoamatsukami "Kotoamatsukami")* (別天津神, "Distinguished Heavenly *Kami*") appeared, who were then followed by [seven generations of deities](/wiki/Kamiyonanayo "Kamiyonanayo"). The seventh and final generation of *kami*, a male-female pair known as [Izanagi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Izanagi "Izanagi") (伊邪那岐命) and [Izanami-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Izanami "Izanami") (伊邪那美命), are ordered to solidify and shape the earth, which was then like floating oil on the primeval ocean. The couple, using a [spear](/wiki/Amenonuhoko "Amenonuhoko"), churn the ocean, thus forming the island of [Onogoro](/wiki/Onogoro_Island "Onogoro Island") (淤能碁呂島).

[The Birth of the Gods](/wiki/Kamiumi "Kamiumi") (神生み *Kamiumi*)
:   Making their home on the island, Izanagi and Izanami marry and beget the islands of Japan as well as numerous other *kami*. Izanami dies while giving birth to the fire god [Kagutsuchi-no-Kami](/wiki/Kagutsuchi "Kagutsuchi") (火神迦具土神); in a fit of rage, Izanagi kills the newborn Kagutsuchi and [searches for his wife](/wiki/Katabasis#Trip_into_the_underworld "Katabasis") in [Yomi](/wiki/Yomi "Yomi") (黄泉国, *Yomi no kuni*), the land of the dead. Izanami reveals that she had already eaten the food of the underworld and thus cannot return to the land of the living; she will, however, try to ask for permission and bade Izanagi to wait. Izanagi loses his patience and lights his comb as a torch to gaze at his wife, only to find that Izanami is now a rotting corpse. Horrified at this sight, Izanagi runs away and seals the entrance to Yomi. Izanami pronounces a curse, vowing to kill a thousand people each day, to which Izanagi replies that he will then beget fifteen hundred people everyday to thwart her. Izanagi, feeling [contaminated](/wiki/Kegare "Kegare") by his visit to Yomi, went to [immerse](/wiki/Misogi "Misogi") himself in a river, bringing more gods into existence as he does so. The three most important *kami*, the "Three Precious Children" (三貴子, *mihashira no uzu no miko*, *sankishi*) – [Amaterasu Ōmikami](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu") (天照大御神), [Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Tsukuyomi "Tsukuyomi") (月読命), and [Susanoo-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Susanoo "Susanoo") (須佐之男命) – came into existence when Izanagi washed his left eye, his right eye, and his nose, respectively.

Amaterasu and Susanoo
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Origin_of_the_Cave_Door_Dance_%28Amaterasu%29_by_Shunsai_Toshimasa_1889.jpg/330px-Origin_of_the_Cave_Door_Dance_%28Amaterasu%29_by_Shunsai_Toshimasa_1889.jpg)](/wiki/File:Origin_of_the_Cave_Door_Dance_(Amaterasu)_by_Shunsai_Toshimasa_1889.jpg)

    Amaterasu emerges out of the Heavenly Rock Cave (Shunsai Toshimasa, 1889)

    Izanagi divides the world among his three children: Amaterasu was allotted [Takamagahara](/wiki/Takamagahara "Takamagahara") (高天原, the "Plain of High Heaven"), Tsukuyomi the night, and Susanoo the seas. Susanoo, who missed his mother and kept weeping and howling incessantly, rejects his appointed task, leading Izanagi to expel him. Susanoo then goes up to Takamagahara, claiming to wish to see his sister. When a suspicious Amaterasu went out to meet him clad in armor, Susanoo protested his innocence and proposed that they [exchange oaths](/wiki/Ukehi "Ukehi"). Five male *kami* (Amaterasu's sons) and [three female *kami*](/wiki/Munakata_Taisha "Munakata Taisha") (Susanoo's daughters) come into existence when the two gods each chewed and spat out an object carried by the other (Amaterasu Susanoo's sword, Susanoo Amaterasu's *[magatama](/wiki/Magatama "Magatama")* beads) during the rite of oath-taking. Susanoo, declaring himself the winner of the contest, "raged with victory" and proceeds to wreak havoc upon Takamagahara, causing Amaterasu to hide in the [Ama-no-Iwato](/wiki/Ama-no-Iwato "Ama-no-Iwato") (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave"), plunging heaven and earth into total darkness. The gods, led by the wise [Omoikane-no-Kami](/wiki/Omoikane_(Shinto) "Omoikane (Shinto)") (思金神), eventually persuade her to come out of the cave, restoring light to the world. As punishment for his misdeeds, Susanoo is thrown out of Takamagahara.

    Susanoo asks the *kami* of food, [Ōgetsuhime-no-Kami](/wiki/%C5%8Cgetsuhime "Ōgetsuhime") (大気都比売神), to give him something to eat. When the goddess [produced foodstuffs](/wiki/Dema_Deity "Dema Deity") from her mouth, nose, and rectum, a disgusted Susanoo kills her, at which various crops, plants and seeds spring from her dead body. Susanoo then makes his way down to [Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni](/wiki/Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni "Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni") (葦原中国, the "Central Land of Reed Plains", i.e. the earthly land of Japan), to the [land of Izumo](/wiki/Izumo_Province "Izumo Province"), where he slays a monstrous eight-headed serpent called the [Yamata-no-Orochi](/wiki/Yamata-no-Orochi "Yamata-no-Orochi") (八俣遠呂智) to rescue the earthly goddess [Kushinadahime](/wiki/Kushinadahime "Kushinadahime") (櫛名田比売), whom he married. Out of the serpent's carcass, Susanoo finds the sword [Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi](/wiki/Kusanagi "Kusanagi") (天叢雲剣, "Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"), which he presents to Amaterasu as a reconciliatory gift.

Ōkuninushi
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Shirousagi_-02.jpg/250px-Shirousagi_-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shirousagi_-02.jpg)

    Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) meets the [Hare of Inaba](/wiki/Hare_of_Inaba "Hare of Inaba")

    A descendant of Susanoo, [Ōnamuji-no-Kami](/wiki/%C5%8Ckuninushi "Ōkuninushi") (大穴牟遅神), helps a hapless [hare](/wiki/Hare_of_Inaba "Hare of Inaba") that had been mistreated by his eighty brothers (八十神, *yasogami*); the hare, in turn, helps Ōnamuji win the hand of the goddess Yagamihime (八上比売) of [Inaba](/wiki/Inaba_Province "Inaba Province"). This earns Ōnamuji the jealousy of his brothers, who begin to make repeated attempts on his life. Seeking refuge in a subterranean realm ruled by Susanoo called [Ne-no-Katasukuni](/wiki/Ne-no-kuni "Ne-no-kuni") (根之堅洲国), Ōnamuji meets and falls in love with Susanoo's daughter Suseribime-no-Mikoto (須勢理毘売). Upon learning of their affair, Susanoo imposes four trials on Ōnamuji, each of which he overcame with Suseribime's help. Ōnamuji manages to outwit Susanoo and leave the realm, taking his new wife Suseribime as well as Susanoo's sword, *[koto](/wiki/Koto_(instrument) "Koto (instrument)")*, and bow and arrows back with him, but not before being advised by Susanoo to change his name to Ōkuninushi-no-Kami (大国主神, "Master of the Great Land"). Ōkuninushi defeats his wicked brothers and becomes the lord of Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni. Under the name Yachihoko-no-Kami (八千矛神, "Eight Thousand Spears"), he takes a third wife, Nunakawahime (沼河比売) of [Koshi](/wiki/Koshi_Province_(Japan) "Koshi Province (Japan)").

    A tiny god riding on the waves of the sea in a bean-pod appears before Ōkuninushi. A god in the form of a [scarecrow](/wiki/Scarecrow "Scarecrow") named [Kuebiko](/wiki/Kuebiko "Kuebiko") (久延毘古) identifies the dwarf as Sukunabikona-no-Kami (少名毘古那神), a son of [Kamimusubi-no-Kami](/wiki/Kamimusubi-no-Kami "Kamimusubi-no-Kami") (神産巣日神), one of the three primordial *Kotoamatsukami*. Sukunabikona assists Ōkuninushi in his task of creating and developing the land (*kuni-zukuri*), but eventually crosses over to the "eternal land" (常世国, *[tokoyo no kuni](/wiki/Tokoyo_no_kuni "Tokoyo no kuni")*), leaving Ōkuninushi without a partner. Another deity, [Ōmononushi-no-Kami](/wiki/%C5%8Cmononushi "Ōmononushi") (大物主神), then appears and promises to aid Ōkuninushi if he will worship him. Ōkuninushi then enshrines Ōmononushi in [Mount Mimoro](/wiki/Mount_Miwa "Mount Miwa") in [Yamato Province](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province").

    [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Bentenjima_on_Inasa_Beach.jpg/250px-Bentenjima_on_Inasa_Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bentenjima_on_Inasa_Beach.jpg)

    Inasa Beach, [Shimane Prefecture](/wiki/Shimane_Prefecture "Shimane Prefecture")

[The Subjugation of Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni](/wiki/Kuni-yuzuri "Kuni-yuzuri")
:   The gods dwelling in Takamagahara decide that Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, which they consider to be overpopulated by unruly and evil *kami*, must be pacified and turned over to their rule. Amaterasu decrees that Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto (天忍穂耳命), one of the five sons born to her when Susanoo chewed her *magatama* beads, shall be the one sent down to take possession of the land. Ame-no-Oshihomimi, after observing the earth below, deems it to be too tumultuous and refuses to go. Another son, Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto (天菩比命) was sent, but ended up siding with Ōkuninushi and did not return for three years. A third messenger, Ame-no-Wakahiko (天若日子), was then dispatched, but ended up marrying Ōkuninushi's daughter Shitateruhime (下照比売) and did not report for eight years, plotting to gain the land for himself. He was eventually slain by the same arrow he used to shoot a [pheasant](/wiki/Pheasant "Pheasant") sent by the heavenly deities to question him. During Ame-no-Wakahiko's funeral, Shitateruhime's brother [Ajishikitakahikone-no-Kami](/wiki/Ajisukitakahikone "Ajisukitakahikone") (阿遅志貴高日子根神) is enraged at being mistaken for the dead god (whom he resembled in appearance) and destroys the mourning house (*moya*) where the funeral was held.
    The heavenly deities then dispatch the god of thunder, [Takemikazuchi-no-Kami](/wiki/Takemikazuchi "Takemikazuchi") (建御雷神), who descends on the shores of Inasa (伊那佐之小浜, *Inasa no ohama*) in Izumo. Ōkuninushi tells Takemikazuchi to confer with his son [Kotoshironushi-no-Kami](/wiki/Kotoshironushi "Kotoshironushi") (事代主神); after being questioned, Kotoshironushi accepts the demands of the heavenly *kami* and withdraws. When Takemikazuchi asks if Ōkuninushi has any other sons who ought to be consulted, Ōkuninushi's second son, [Takeminakata-no-Kami](/wiki/Takeminakata "Takeminakata") (建御名方神), appears and brashly challenges Takemikazuchi to a test of strength but is defeated. Takeminakata flees to [the sea of Suwa](/wiki/Lake_Suwa "Lake Suwa") in [Shinano](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province"), where he finally surrenders. After hearing that his two sons have submitted, Ōkuninushi relinquishes his control of the land, asking only that [a magnificent palace](/wiki/Izumo-taisha "Izumo-taisha") be built in his honor. Ōkuninushi withdraws into the unseen world, while Takemikazuchi returns to Takamagahara, his mission completed.

[The Descent of Ninigi, the Heavenly Grandson](/wiki/Tenson_k%C5%8Drin "Tenson kōrin") ([天孫降臨](/wiki/%E5%A4%A9%E5%AD%AB%E9%99%8D%E8%87%A8 "天孫降臨") *Tenson kōrin*)
:   Ame-no-Oshihomimi is once again commanded to descend to Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni in order to take possession of it, at which Ame-no-Oshihomimi recommends that his son, [Hikoho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Ninigi "Ninigi") (日子番能邇邇芸命), be sent instead. Amaterasu bequeaths to Ninigi [three sacred treasures](/wiki/Imperial_Regalia_of_Japan "Imperial Regalia of Japan") – the sword Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙剣, "Grass-cutter", another name for the Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi) given to her by Susanoo, the [mirror](/wiki/Yata_no_Kagami "Yata no Kagami") used by the other gods to lure her out of the Ama-no-Iwato, and [*magatama* beads](/wiki/Magatama#In_Japanese_mythology "Magatama") – and has a number of gods (which included the five divine ancestors of the priestly clans involved in the services of the imperial family) accompany him in his descent. As Ninigi was about to come down, an earthly deity named [Sarutabiko-no-Kami](/wiki/Sarutahiko "Sarutahiko") (猿田毘古神) comes and offers to be his guide. Ninigi finally descends from heaven to the summit of Mount [Takachiho](/wiki/Takachiho,_Miyazaki "Takachiho, Miyazaki") in [Himuka](/wiki/Hy%C5%ABga_Province "Hyūga Province") and sets up his dwelling there. He meets a goddess named [Kohohana-no-Sakuyabime](/wiki/Konohanasakuya-hime "Konohanasakuya-hime") (木花之佐久夜毘売, "Princess of the Flowering Trees"), the daughter of [Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami](/wiki/%C5%8Cyamatsumi "Ōyamatsumi") (大山津見神), the god of mountains, and seeks to marry her. Ōyamatsumi approves of the marriage and also offers his elder daughter Iwanagahime (石長比売 "Princess of the Eternal Rocks") to Ninigi, but Ninigi rejects her due to her ugliness. Ōyamatsumi declares that he had sent his two daughters to Ninigi to ensure that Ninigi would endure like the rocks and flourish like the trees and flowers; however, because Ninigi had rejected Iwanagahime in favor of Sakuyabime, he is doomed to live a life as fleeting as the blossoms of the trees. This, the story explains, is why the emperors – Ninigi's descendants – are mortal.
    Sakuyabime becomes pregnant after spending only one night with Ninigi, causing suspicion in Ninigi. To prove her fidelity, Sakuyabime confines herself inside a sealed birth hut and sets it ablaze as she was about to give birth. She delivered three children in the midst of the fire: [Hoderi-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Hoderi "Hoderi") (火照命), Hosuseri-no-Mikoto (火須勢理命), and [Hoori-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Hoori "Hoori") (火遠理命).

Hoori (Yamasachihiko)
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Hoori_and_Toyotama-hime.jpg/250px-Hoori_and_Toyotama-hime.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hoori_and_Toyotama-hime.jpg)

    Hoori and Toyotamabime

    Hoderi and Hoori, also known as Umisachihiko (海幸彦, "Luck of the Sea") and Yamasachihiko (山幸彦, "Luck of the Mountain"), grew up to be a fisherman and a hunter, respectively. One time, the brothers agree to exchange places and try using the other's tools. Hoori ended up losing his brother's fish hook in the sea; he tries to make compensation, but Hoderi, furious, insists on having the original hook. Hoori goes to the sea in search of the fish hook and ends up in the palace of the sea god, [Watatsumi-no-Kami](/wiki/Watatsumi "Watatsumi") (綿津見神), where he is warmly welcomed, and marries Watatsumi's daughter, [Toyotamabime](/wiki/Toyotamahime "Toyotamahime") (豊玉毘売). After staying in Watatsumi's realm for three years, Hoori expresses his wish to return to the surface. The lost fish hook is finally discovered lodged in a [sea bream](/wiki/Pagrus_major "Pagrus major")'s throat. Watatsumi gives Hoori two magical jewels: the *Shiomitsutama* (塩盈珠, "Tide-raising Jewel") and the *Shiofurutama* (塩乾珠, "Tide-ebbing Jewel"), telling him to use both to subdue his brother. Hoori goes back to land on the back of a *[wani](/wiki/Wani_(dragon) "Wani (dragon)")* and returns the fish hook to Hoderi, now cursed to give bad luck to its user. Within three years, Hoderi became poverty-stricken and attempted to attack Hoori, who then used the two jewels to defeat him. Finally, Hoderi submitted and swore fealty to Hoori.

Ugayafukiaezu
:   Toyotamabime arrives on the surface pregnant with Hoori's child, wishing to give birth on land. A birth hut thatched with cormorant feathers was built for her. Toyotamahime tells Hoori not to look at her while she is giving birth; however, he breaks his promise and discovers her true form to be that of a *wani*. Angry and ashamed at having her true form discovered, she returned to the sea and never met him again. Hoori reigned in the palace at Takachiho for 580 years before his death.
    Toyotamabime entrusted her newborn son, whom she named [Ugayafukiaezu-no-Mikoto](/wiki/Ugayafukiaezu "Ugayafukiaezu") (鵜葦草不合命, "Unfinished Cormorant-Feather Thatching"), to the care of her sister, [Tamayoribime](/wiki/Tamayori-hime_(mother_of_Jimmu) "Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)") (玉依毘売命). Ugayafukiaezu eventually took his aunt Tamayoribime as his wife and had four children with her, one of whom was Kamu-Yamato-Iwarebiko-no-Mikoto (神倭伊波礼毘古命), also known as the [Emperor Jimmu](/wiki/Emperor_Jimmu "Emperor Jimmu").

* **Takeohirokunioshitate no mikoto (建小広国押楯命), or [Emperor Senka](/wiki/Emperor_Senka "Emperor Senka") (宣化天皇)**
* **Amekunioshiharukihironiwa no sumeramiko (天国押波琉岐広庭天皇), or [Emperor Kinmei](/wiki/Emperor_Kinmei "Emperor Kinmei") (欽明天皇)**
* **Nunakurafutotamashiki no mikoto (沼名倉太玉敷命), or [Emperor Bidatsu](/wiki/Emperor_Bidatsu "Emperor Bidatsu") (敏達天皇)**
* **Tachibananotoyohi no miko (橘豊日王), or [Emperor Yōmei](/wiki/Emperor_Y%C5%8Dmei "Emperor Yōmei") (用明天皇)**
* **Hatsusebenowakasazaki no sumeramikoto (長谷部若雀天皇), or [Emperor Sushun](/wiki/Emperor_Sushun "Emperor Sushun") (崇峻天皇)**
* **Toyomikekashikiyahime no mikoto (豊御食炊屋比売命), or [Empress Suiko](/wiki/Empress_Suiko "Empress Suiko") (推古天皇)**
